description: A household counts its jars before winter; a group counts who has arrived. 'tawi' enumerates distinguishable people, things, or events, either exactly or by a contextual approximation. 'masue' (measure) compares a magnitude with a standard instead of saying how many members a set contains.
articulatory_notes: Make unvoiced dental 't' by sealing the tongue tip against the backs of the upper incisors, then release into stressed central 'a'. For 'wi', round the lips and lift the tongue back for the voiced glide before moving forward and unrounding into high-front 'i'.
sound_symbolism: The word places 'ta' (one) before 'wi' (two), so its two syllables perform the beginning of a count. This is deliberate audible kinship with Phi's first positive numerals.
usage_notes: The counted set or its members precede the verb. Exact Phi numerals run from zero through 242; a bare scale or ordinary quantifier gives an approximation. Larger exact values and technically formatted tallies stay in their source form when the notation matters. 'wia' asks how many.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: An evening count of animals or a seasonal pantry tally can reveal a loss before hunger or weather makes it urgent. The verb names the enumeration and leaves its purpose open.

semantic_domains.activity: enumerating the members of a set for a practical purpose
semantic_domains.cognition: keeping track of successive items during enumeration
semantic_domains.quantity: determining how many members a set contains
